items.q: Should an anomalous point simply be included in the line of best fit?
items.correct: No — identify it, repeat or investigate it, and exclude it from the fit if confirmed anomalous.
items.misconception: Yes — every measured point must influence the line equally.
items.random_wrong: No — delete it secretly without comment.
items.why: Students force lines through anomalies, distorting gradients; good practice is to identify and justify exclusion.

items.q: How do you reduce the effect of reaction time when timing oscillations with a stopwatch?
items.correct: Time many oscillations and divide by the number of cycles to find one period.
items.why: Students try to react quicker, but reaction error is roughly fixed; spreading it over many cycles shrinks its effect per cycle.

items.q: Is the gradient of a graph found from two of your plotted data points?
items.correct: No — use two widely separated points on the best-fit line itself.
items.misconception: Yes — pick any two of your measured points.
items.random_wrong: No — gradients are read directly off the y-axis.
items.why: Using raw data points reintroduces their random error; the best-fit line has already averaged out the scatter.

items.q: To avoid parallax error when reading a liquid level, you should:
items.correct: Read the scale at eye level, perpendicular to it.
items.random_wrong: Take the reading as quickly as possible.
items.why: Correct answer: Read the scale at eye level, perpendicular to it.. “Use a wider measuring cylinder.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: You vary the voltage across a lamp and measure the current. The dependent variable is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The current. “The voltage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why draw a line of best fit rather than joining the points dot-to-dot?
items.correct: It averages out random scatter to reveal the underlying trend.
items.misconception: It makes the graph look longer.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It averages out random scatter to reveal the underlying trend.. “It makes the graph look longer.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A micrometer reads +0.04 mm with its jaws fully closed. You should:
items.correct: Subtract 0.04 mm from each reading to correct the zero error.
items.random_wrong: Ignore it because it is small.
items.why: It's Subtract 0.04 mm from each reading to correct the zero error.. “Add 0.04 mm to each reading.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A timer records repeats of 2.46 s, 2.50 s and 2.54 s. What is the mean time?
items.why: The real figure is 2.5 s. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
